channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Women Who Fled Iran Are to Be Deported to Central African Republic, Lawyers Say
item.description: The women are among nearly two dozen people slated to be sent to a country where the U.S. government has advised “Do not travel for any reason.”
item.dc:creator: Megha Rajagopalan and Hamed Aleaziz
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration is working to find ways to deport some migrants to third countries as a way around court orders barring their return home.

item.title: Analysis of Satellite Image and Videos Suggest Precision U.S. Strikes on Iranian Water Facility
item.description: It is unclear if the U.S. intentionally struck the facility or knew what it was. Deliberately targeting civilian infrastructure could constitute a war crime.
item.dc:creator: Christoph Koettl and Christiaan Triebert
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: With Inflation Bearing Down, Europe Is Facing Higher Interest Rates
item.description: The European Central Bank is set to raise rates, the first such move since September 2023, as energy disruptions caused by the Iran war drive higher prices.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times
item.media:description: The European Central Bank is expected to raise its forecast for inflation as the war pushes prices higher.

item.title: Trump Shrugs Off Surging Inflation, and Why a Groundbreaking Social Media Ban Is Floundering
item.description: Plus, the man behind “GOOOOAL!”
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Victoria Kim
item.media:description: As inflation outpaces workers’ wages, President Trump and his administration insist that their agenda is working.

item.title: The Knicks’ Game 4 Comeback Was Epic. So Was Their Fans’ Response.
item.description: The crowd at Madison Square Garden was forlorn as the New York Knicks fell far behind in Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals. After they won, it was electric.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: How a High-Powered Gathering Turned Into a Knicks Watch Party
item.description: An event for some of New York’s elite shifted gears when a TV rolled into the bar.
item.dc:creator: Dionne Searcey and Adrienne Grunwald
item.media:description: Watching Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals beside a candlelit spread.

item.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A talk on rising energy costs on Capitol Hill hosted by Democrats, from left, Sen. Martin Heinrich of New Mexico, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, in March.

item.title: A Youthful Crop of Politicians Run on Housing, and Their Own Rent
item.description: A cohort of young Millennial and Gen Z politicians have centered their campaigns this year on housing costs, and the divide is more generational than partisan.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Morales, 27, a candidate for mayor of Providence, R.I., has made housing central to his pitch for “a city all our neighbors can afford.”

item.title: Mace Defeat Heralds an Exodus of Rabble-Rousers From Congress
item.description: The Republican congresswoman from South Carolina, who made enemies on both sides of the political aisle, is just one of several flashy lawmakers to be leaving Capitol Hill.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Nancy Mace, Republican of South Carolina, at the Capitol earlier this year.

item.title: The War in Ukraine Has Now Gone On Longer Than World War I
item.description: Parallels between the two wars abound, from the grinding nature of the fighting to the way new technologies reshaped warfare.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: A Ukrainian soldier in Kostiantynivka, a city ravaged by drone warfare, in January.

item.title: 29 Shows to Watch This Summer
item.description: “The Bear” serves its last course, “Ted Lasso” gets back on the pitch, and Larry David makes a TV show with the Obamas.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: “Ted Lasso” returns in August with, center, from left, Brendan Hunt, Jason Sudeikis and Tanya Reynolds.

item.title: A Fractured North America Begins a Historic World Cup
item.description: Mexico and Canada have faced significant recent tension in their relations with the United States. But in their historic World Cup bid, the three countries promoted teamwork.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mexico City’s iconic stadium will host the opener for a record third time, after undergoing a near $200 million makeover for this World Cup and increasing its capacity to 87,500 seats.

item.title: Conor McGregor’s Comeback: A Tale of Banned Drugs and a Famous Doctor
item.description: McGregor, the Ultimate Fighting Championship’s main attraction, had the support of the prominent sports physician Neal ElAttrache when he decided to use performance-enhancing drugs.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:description: Conor McGregor preparing to fight Dustin Poirier in Las Vegas in 2021. McGregor suffered a badly broken leg during the bout.

item.title: How Tesla’s Stock Listing in 2010 Enabled SpaceX’s I.P.O.
item.description: Investors who bought the automaker’s shares in its initial public offering became rich. As a result, many have a deep faith in Elon Musk, the chief executive of Tesla and SpaceX.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: During Tesla’s initial public offering in 2010, Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive, said, “The smartest money in the world is betting on Tesla.”

item.title: Why It’s Nearly Impossible to Build a Robot Without China
item.description: Building on the country’s electric vehicle industry, Chinese companies are making robot parts at a scale and price point others can’t match.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times

item.title: Microsoft C.E.O. Satya Nadella Says ‘Everyone Is a Stakeholder’ in A.I.
item.description: At The New York Times’s Hard Fork Live event, Mr. Nadella addressed the backlash against artificial intelligence and President Trump’s comments about Americans sharing in the wealth of A.I. companies
item.dc:creator: Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Satya Nadella, left, the chief executive of Microsoft, at the Hard Fork Live event in San Francisco on Wednesday.

item.title: A.I. and the Chip Boom: Take a Quiz on New Korean Slang and Memes
item.description: What’s a “silicon-collar”? South Korea’s chip industry has been supercharged by the advent of the A.I. age, inspiring a host of new phrases.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim

item.title: In a Trump-Endorsed Presidential Candidate, Colombia’s Women See a Familiar Far-Right Playbook
item.description: Colombia’s presidential front-runner has sparked a debate about masculinity and machismo. Women’s rights groups see a familiar right-wing playbook.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The presidential candidate Abelardo De La Espriella during an election rally last month in Medellín, Colombia.

item.title: U.S. Officials Told Colombia to Cancel President’s Meeting With Mamdani
item.description: The State Department canceled President Gustavo Petro’s visa last year after he attended a pro-Palestinian rally in Manhattan. He had planned to attend a forum led by Mayor Mamdani of New York.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Edward Wong and Jeffery C. Mays
item.media:description: President Gustavo Petro of Colombia during a United Nations Security Council meeting in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: A Newer Approach to Editing Embryos Ignites Debate
item.description: Fertility specialists, biotech companies and ethicists are divided over whether progress in early gene editing would wipe out diseases or trigger a rush toward enhancement.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:description: A colored light micrograph of a human embryo 48 to 72 hours after in vitro fertilization. “Human embryos become living human persons,” one bioethicist said. “The research is being done on future persons without their consent.”

item.title: Inside Trump’s White House, the Epstein Files Caused a Freakout
item.description: The president’s top advisers gathered in a series of Situation Room meetings as they struggled to contain a scandal engulfing Donald Trump himself.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Top Pentagon Official Worked Closely With C.I.A. Officer Later Found With Gold Bars
item.description: David Rush, the C.I.A. employee, worked on a highly classified China spying program with Stephen A. Feinberg, the Pentagon’s second-ranking official.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Stephen A. Feinberg in February 2025, during his confirmation hearing to be deputy defense secretary.

item.title: She Was One of Obama’s Top Lawyers. How Did She Get Tangled Up With Epstein?
item.description: That she, of all people, looked past his 2008 conviction tells you everything you need to know about how unreliable and corroded the legal world has become.
item.dc:creator: Ankush Khardori
item.media:credit: Cait Oppermann for The New York Times

item.title: Better Sex, Better Hair, Better Sleep: ‘Humanmaxxing’ Is Here
item.description: The biotech billionaire who wants to rebuild your body and blow your mind.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Victoria Chamberlin

item.title: The World Cup
item.description: Here’s what you should know about this massive competition.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Desi Lydic Does a Spit Take at the Price of Trump’s Gold Coin
item.description: “$12,000? For a coin? Does it come with a used Honda Civic?” Lydic said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: The Mystery Artist Filling Subway Ad Space With Whimsy
item.description: Sue Sarah Gilbert, a Rockefeller descendant in Seattle, raised $1 million to place her drawings in New York City stations.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen
item.media:credit: Michelle V. Agins/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sue Sarah Gilbert, a self-described hobby artist, raised money from investors and contributed some of her own to fund a monthslong campaign displaying her artwork in several subway stations.
